questions.stem: A child arrives with fever, drooling, muffled voice, and inspiratory stridor. Which action by the nurse is the priority?

options.text: Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management.
Inspect the throat with a tongue blade for redness.
Obtain a throat culture before notifying the provider.
Lay the child flat for a full respiratory assessment.

questions.rationale: Drooling, stridor, and a muffled voice suggest epiglottitis, which can lead to sudden airway obstruction. The nurse should keep the child calm, avoid upsetting airway manipulation, and prepare for expert airway management. Using a tongue blade or forcing the child flat can precipitate complete obstruction.

distractorRationales.b: Inspect the throat with a tongue blade for redness misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Obtain a throat culture before notifying the provider misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Lay the child flat for a full respiratory assessment misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Suspected epiglottitis is an airway emergency: do not inspect the throat and do prepare for airway management.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A laboring client receiving oxytocin has recurrent late fetal heart rate decelerations. Which nursing action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the oxytocin infusion.
Prepare for an immediate vaginal examination.
Increase the oxytocin to improve labor progress.
Encourage the client to begin pushing with each contraction.

questions.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations suggest uteroplacental insufficiency, and oxytocin can worsen that pattern by increasing uterine activity. The first nursing move is to stop the oxytocin, then reposition the client, increase IV fluids, and notify the provider as needed. Increasing stimulation or encouraging pushing can further reduce fetal oxygen delivery.

distractorRationales.b: Prepare for an immediate vaginal examination misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Increase the oxytocin to improve labor progress misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Encourage the client to begin pushing with each contraction misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Late decelerations with oxytocin on board mean remove the stressor first by stopping the infusion.

questions.stem: A postpartum client receiving magnesium sulfate for preeclampsia has respirations of 10/min, absent patellar reflexes, and urine output of 20 mL/hr. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider.
Administer the next scheduled antihypertensive dose.
Place the client flat and encourage rest.
Reassess reflexes after the client has been sleeping for 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression, absent reflexes, and oliguria are classic toxicity findings with magnesium sulfate. The drug should be stopped immediately and the provider notified so antidote and further management can be started. Waiting or focusing on less urgent medications allows magnesium levels to continue rising and increases the risk of arrest.

distractorRationales.b: Administer the next scheduled antihypertensive dose misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client flat and encourage rest misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Reassess reflexes after the client has been sleeping for 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Low respirations, absent reflexes, and low urine output on magnesium sulfate point to toxicity and require the infusion to be stopped first.

questions.stem: A postoperative client received IV morphine 20 minutes ago. The nurse finds the client difficult to arouse with respirations of 8/min. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone.
Document that the client is sleeping after pain relief.
Place a warm blanket on the client and reassess in 30 minutes.
Ask the client to rate pain before intervening.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression after opioid administration is a priority safety emergency. The nurse should stop additional opioid exposure, stimulate the client, support breathing, and prepare naloxone per protocol. Comfort-focused reassessment without addressing respirations delays treatment of an unstable airway and ventilation problem.

distractorRationales.b: Document that the client is sleeping after pain relief misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Place a warm blanket on the client and reassess in 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Ask the client to rate pain before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Difficulty arousing plus slow respirations after opioids means act for oversedation before anything else.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis
A patient asking for a blanket
A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow
A patient requesting help finding a charger

questions.rationale: Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent requests.

distractorRationales.b: A patient asking for a blanket misses the clinical pattern: Stable Vs Unstable pain points to A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow misses the clinical pattern: Stable Vs Unstable pain points to A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: A patient requesting help finding a charger misses the clinical pattern: Stable Vs Unstable pain points to A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.

questions.stem: A pregnant patient at 31 weeks presents with epigastric/RUQ pain, headache, nausea, and malaise. Labs reveal: AST 280 U/L, LDH 950 U/L, platelets 74,000/mm3, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL with schistocytes on smear, and normal creatinine. Which condition do these findings indicate?

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)
HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets)
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia

questions.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a variant of severe preeclampsia: Hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low Hgb), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST >70 U/L), Low Platelets (<100,000). RUQ/epigastric pain from hepatic capsule distension is classic. Definitive treatment is delivery. AFLP (A) presents with marked coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR, hypoglycemia, jaundice) due to hepatocyte fat infiltration — different mechanism. TTP (C) presents with the pentad including fever and renal failure. Viral hepatitis (D) would not present with schistocytes and would have different epidemiology.

distractorRationales.a: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia misses the clinical pattern: Hellp Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: HELLP = Hemolysis (schistocytes + elevated LDH) + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets; treatment is delivery regardless of gestational age.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old patient with bipolar disorder refuses psychiatric hospitalization. He has not slept in 5 days, believes he is receiving messages from aliens, and stated he plans to 'make his neighbor pay' for 'spying on him.' Which criterion most legally supports involuntary psychiatric commitment?

options.text: Presence of a psychiatric diagnosis requiring treatment
Refusal of recommended medication
Danger to others based on specific expressed threat
Inability to care for personal hygiene and nutrition

questions.rationale: Involuntary psychiatric commitment (civil commitment) requires that the person poses a danger to self, danger to others, or is gravely disabled (unable to meet basic needs of food, clothing, shelter due to mental illness). This patient's specific threat to 'make his neighbor pay' combined with active psychosis meets the danger-to-others criterion. Having a psychiatric diagnosis alone (A) is not sufficient for commitment. Medication refusal (B) alone does not meet criteria. While grave disability (D) could apply, the most legally defensible criterion here is the specific threat to another person.

distractorRationales.a: Presence of a psychiatric diagnosis requiring treatment misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.b: Refusal of recommended medication misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Inability to care for personal hygiene and nutrition misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Involuntary commitment requires: danger to self, danger to others (specific threat + mental illness), or grave disability — psychiatric diagnosis alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100.8 F (38.2 C), tenderness over the graft site, and decreased urine output. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection.
Reassure the client that mild fever is expected after transplant.
Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose.
Delay action until the next routine clinic visit.

questions.rationale: Fever, graft tenderness, and decreased urine output after kidney transplant are concerning for acute rejection. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and intervention. Missed or delayed response can threaten graft survival.

distractorRationales.b: Reassure the client that mild fever is expected after transplant misses the clinical pattern: Acute Rejection Transplant kidney points to Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose misses the clinical pattern: Acute Rejection Transplant kidney points to Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Delay action until the next routine clinic visit misses the clinical pattern: Acute Rejection Transplant kidney points to Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Graft tenderness with falling urine output after transplant is a report-now pattern.

questions.stem: During labor a client suddenly becomes short of breath, cyanotic, and hypotensive. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen.
Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass.
Obtain a routine pain score before intervening.
Document the event as an anxiety reaction.

questions.rationale: Sudden hypoxia and hypotension during labor raise concern for amniotic fluid embolism, a catastrophic obstetric emergency. The nurse should escalate immediately and support oxygenation and circulation. Delay can rapidly endanger both the client and fetus.

distractorRationales.b: Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass misses the clinical pattern: Amniotic Fluid Embolism circulation points to Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Obtain a routine pain score before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Amniotic Fluid Embolism circulation points to Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event as an anxiety reaction misses the clinical pattern: Amniotic Fluid Embolism circulation points to Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Sudden maternal collapse in labor requires immediate emergency response and oxygenation support.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: Obtain a full allergy history before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A client rescued from a house fire has singed nasal hair, soot around the mouth, and new hoarseness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen.
Apply topical burn cream to the face before any other action.
Encourage the client to drink water to soothe the throat.
Document the findings and reassess at the end of the shift.

questions.rationale: Hoarseness, soot exposure, and singed nasal hair suggest inhalation injury with potential airway edema. The nurse should prioritize airway protection early rather than waiting for visible obstruction. Delayed action can make intubation much more difficult once swelling progresses.

distractorRationales.b: Apply topical burn cream to the face before any other action misses the clinical pattern: Burn Inhalation Stridor airway points to Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage the client to drink water to soothe the throat misses the clinical pattern: Burn Inhalation Stridor airway points to Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the findings and reassess at the end of the shift misses the clinical pattern: Burn Inhalation Stridor airway points to Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Smoke inhalation clues plus hoarseness mean protect the airway early.
